taskDescription: Click on the key embryological terms in the text below.
textField:
During the third week of development, the *primitive* *streak* appears on Day 14 in the caudal midline of the *embryonic* disc. This marks the beginning of *gastrulation*, which converts the *bilaminar* disc into a *trilaminar* disc consisting of *ectoderm*, *mesoderm*, and *endoderm*. The *primitive* *node*, also called Hensen's node, at the cranial end of the primitive streak acts as the organiser of the embryo.

The *notochord* develops from cells that migrate cranially from the primitive node. It induces the overlying ectoderm to form the *neural* *plate*, initiating *neurulation*. The neural plate folds to form the *neural* *tube*, which gives rise to the brain and spinal cord. Failure of *cranial* closure causes *anencephaly*, while failure of *caudal* closure causes *spina* *bifida*.

The *somites* begin to appear from Day 20 as segmented blocks of *paraxial* mesoderm. They differentiate into the *sclerotome* (vertebrae), *myotome* (skeletal muscles), and *dermatome* (dermis). The embryonic period extends from the 3rd to the 8th week, after which organogenesis is largely complete and the developing human is termed a *fetus*.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the embryological development material and try again.
